पत्र – पत्रकार रवीश कुमार जी के नाम
 विषय – सिंगरौली (म.प्र.) में कोयला कंपनियों द्वारा हो रही लगातार दुर्घटनाएं और पीड़ित परिवारों की आवाज़ को दबाया जाना
 
 आदरणीय रवीश कुमार जी,
 सादर नमस्कार।
 
 मैं मध्यप्रदेश के सिंगरौली ज़िले से एक सामान्य नागरिक होकर आपको यह पत्र लिख रहा हूँ। यहाँ पर देश की नामी-गिरामी कंपनियाँ जैसे अडानी, एनटीपीसी, कोल इंडिया, आदि कोयला खनन का कार्य करती हैं। इन कंपनियों के द्वारा चलने वाले भारी वाहन जैसे हाइवा, डंपर, ट्रक आदि, रोज़ सड़कों पर दौड़ते हैं। लेकिन विडंबना यह है कि ये वाहन रोज किसी न किसी मासूम का जीवन छीन रहे हैं।
 
 गाँवों से गुज़रते हुए ये वाहन बच्चों, बुज़ुर्गों और महिलाओं को कुचल देते हैं। कई बार पूरे परिवार तबाह हो चुके हैं। लेकिन हर बार कंपनी कुछ पैसे देकर मामला शांत करा देती है। पीड़ित परिवारों को मजबूर किया जाता है कि वे मुंह बंद रखें, और जो आवाज़ उठाता है, उसे धमकाया जाता है।
 
 यहाँ के स्थानीय नेता, विशेषकर सत्ताधारी दल से जुड़े विधायक और उनके गुंडे, कंपनियों के दलाल बने हुए हैं। जो लोग न्याय की मांग करते हैं, उन्हें या तो डरा दिया जाता है या फिर झूठे मुकदमों में फंसा दिया जाता है।
 
 हमारे पास न मीडिया है, न मंच है, और न ही इतना साहस कि बड़े-बड़े पूंजीपतियों और राजनेताओं से लड़ सकें। लेकिन आपके जैसे निष्पक्ष पत्रकार हमारे जैसे ग़रीबों की आख़िरी उम्मीद हैं।
 
 आपसे निवेदन है कि कृपया एक बार सिंगरौली की इस भयावह सच्चाई को अपनी रिपोर्टिंग का विषय बनाइए। ताकि देश को यह पता चल सके कि विकास के नाम पर यहाँ के नागरिकों की ज़िंदगियाँ कुचली जा रही हैं।
 
 आपके जवाब और आवाज़ का हम बेसब्री से इंतज़ार करेंगे।

The word gospel literally means “good news” and occurs 93 times in the Bible, exclusively in the New Testament. In Greek, it is the word euaggelion, from which we get our English words evangelist, evangel, and evangelical. The gospel is, broadly speaking, the whole of Scripture; more narrowly, the gospel is the good news concerning Christ and the way of salvation.
 
 ￼
 
 
 The key to understanding the gospel is to know why it’s good news. To do that, we must start with the bad news. The Old Testament Law was given to Israel during the time of Moses (Deuteronomy 5:1). The Law can be thought of as a measuring stick, and sin is anything that falls short of “perfect” according to that standard. The righteous requirement of the Law is so stringent that no human being could possibly follow it perfectly, in letter or in spirit. Despite our “goodness” or “badness” relative to each other, we are all in the same spiritual boat—we have sinned, and the punishment for sin is death, i.e. separation from God, the source of life (Romans 3:23). In order for us to go to heaven, God’s dwelling place and the realm of life and light, sin must be somehow removed or paid for. The Law established the fact that cleansing from sin can only happen through the bloody sacrifice of an innocent life (Hebrews 9:22).
 
 The gospel involves Jesus’ death on the cross as the sin offering to fulfill the Law’s righteous requirement (Romans 8:3–4; Hebrews 10:5–10). Under the Law, animal sacrifices were offered year after year as a reminder of sin and a symbol of the coming sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 10:3–4). When Christ offered Himself at Calvary, that symbol became a reality for all who would believe (Hebrews 10:11–18). The work of atonement is finished now, and that’s good news.
 
 The gospel also involves Jesus’ resurrection on the third day. “He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification” (Romans 4:25). The fact that Jesus conquered sin and death (sin’s penalty) is good news, indeed. The fact that He offers to share that victory with us is the greatest news of all (John 14:19).
 
 The elements of the gospel are clearly stated in 1 Corinthians 15:3–6, a key passage concerning the good news of God: “For what I received I passed on to you as of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, and then to the Twelve. After that, he appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at the same time, most of whom are still living.” Notice, first, that Paul “received” the gospel and then “passed it on”; this is a divine message, not a man-made invention. Second, the gospel is “of first importance.” Everywhere the apostles went, they preached the crucifixion and resurrection of Christ. Third, the message of the gospel is accompanied by proofs: Christ died for our sins (proved by His burial), and He rose again the third day (proved by the eyewitnesses). Fourth, all this was done “according to the Scriptures”; the theme of the whole Bible is the salvation of mankind through Christ. The Bible is the gospel.
 
 “I am not ashamed of the gospel, because it is the power of God that brings salvation to everyone who believes: first to the Jew, then to the Gentile” (Romans 1:16). The gospel is a bold message, and we are not ashamed of proclaiming it. It is a powerful message, because it is God’s good news. It is a saving message, the only thing that can truly reform the human heart. It is a universal message, for Jews and Gentiles both. And the gospel is received by faith; salvation is the gift of God (Ephesians 2:8–9).
 
 The gospel is the good news that God loves the world enough to give His only Son to die for our sin (John 3:16). The gospel is good news because our salvation and eternal life and home in heaven are guaranteed through Christ (John 14:1–4). “He has given us new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, and into an inheritance that can never perish, spoil or fade. This inheritance is kept in heaven for you” (1 Peter 1:3–4).
 
 The gospel is good news when we understand that we do not (and cannot) earn our salvation; the work of redemption and justification is complete, having been finished on the cross (John 19:30). Jesus is the propitiation for our sins (1 John 2:2). The gospel is the good news that we, who were once enemies of God, have been reconciled by the blood of Christ and adopted into the family of God (Romans 5:10; John 1:12). “See what great love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called children of God! And that is what we are!” (1 John 3:1). The gospel is the good news that “there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1).
 
 To reject the gospel is to embrace the bad news. Condemnation before God is the result of a lack of faith in the Son of God, God’s only provision for salvation. “For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through him. Whoever believes in him is not condemned, but whoever does not believe stands condemned already because they have not believed in the name of God’s one and only Son” (John 3:17–18). God has given a doomed world good news: the Gospel of Jesus Christ!

Data Comparison (Focusing on 2014-2024 trends):
 
 Under BJP, India has done significantly better than the UK, France and even Ireland when you think about mass violence and rioting. 
 
 When you compare population size vs crime it's beautiful to see India doing so much better than they were under Congress AND we MUST keep BJP in power to carry on the trend. 
 
 Ignore what Western media and Fascist paid liars call you or how they portray you with their bias, YOU should all be proud. This is the real India! 
 
 1. India: Communal Violence (NCRB Data)
 
 * Context: India's population is over 1.4 billion.
 
 * Data (Communal Riots):
 * 2014: 644 incidents
 * 2015: 789 incidents
 * 2016: 703 incidents
 * 2017: 822 incidents (Peak)
 * 2018: 512 incidents
 * 2019: 438 incidents
 * 2020: 358 incidents
 * 2021: 378 incidents
 * 2022: 272 incidents
 * 2023: 32 incidents (CSSS report)
 * 2024: 59 incidents (CSSS report) 
 
 Note: CSSS data is from an NGO, but widely cited. 
 NCRB 2023/2024 official data not yet fully available.
 
 * Per Capita: When you divide these numbers by 1.4 billion people, the per capita incidence of formally recorded "communal riots" in India is extremely low. 
 
 Even at the peak of 822 incidents in 2017, this is less than 1 incident per 1.7 million people.
 
 2. UK: Riots, Grooming Gangs, and Hate Crime
 
 * Context: UK population is approximately 68 million. 
 
 * Riots/Public Disorder:
 
 * The UK has not experienced nationwide riots on the scale of the 2011 riots within the 2014-2024 period, but significant localized disturbances have occurred.
 
 * 2024 UK Riots: In July-August 2024, there were "estimated 29 anti-immigration demonstrations and riots" across 27 towns and cities, with "1,280 people arrested." 
 These involved attacks on mosques and hotels housing asylum seekers, fueled by far-right, Islamophobic, and racist sentiments. 
 
 This was described as the "most significant disorder in the UK since 2011."
 
 * Other localized protests and clashes occur, but are not typically aggregated as "communal violence."
 
 * Grooming Gangs (Child Sexual Exploitation):
 
 * This is a serious issue. While ethnicity is not consistently recorded nationally, high-profile cases in areas like Rotherham, Rochdale, and Bradford have involved perpetrators predominantly of Pakistani heritage.
 
 * Baroness Casey's audit (2025) noted that ethnicity data is not recorded for two-thirds of perpetrators, making national conclusions difficult. 
 However, in specific areas of concern (Greater Manchester, West Yorkshire, South Yorkshire), "suspects of group-based child sexual offences were disproportionately likely to be Asian men."
 
 * Crucial Classification: These are classified as severe sexual offenses, not "communal violence." 
 
 The victims are primarily vulnerable British children, often white, and the crime is sexual exploitation, not necessarily inter-communal conflict in the direct sense.
 
 * Hate Crime (Religious):
 
 * In the year ending March 2024, there were 10,484 religious hate crime offences recorded in England and Wales. This is a significant increase from previous years. Muslims and Jewish people were disproportionately targeted, but all religious groups can be victims.
 
 * Per Capita: 10,484 religious hate crimes in a population of ~68 million is roughly 15.4 incidents per 100,000 people. This is a much higher per capita rate than India's communal riots. 
 
 3. France: Riots and Social Unrest
 
 * Context: France's population is approximately 65 million. 
 
 * Riots: France has experienced significant and often widespread riots, particularly linked to police violence, social inequality, and ethnic tensions.
 
 * Nahel Merzouk Riots (June 2023): Sparked by the police killing of a teenager of North African origin, these riots spread across the country, causing over $1 billion in damage, involving widespread arson, looting, and clashes with police. 
 This was a major event, often seen as reflecting underlying racial and social tensions in French suburbs (banlieues).
 
 * Yellow Vest Protests (2018-2019): While broader anti-government protests, they often involved significant violence and clashes with police across the country.
 
 * Per Capita: The scale and geographic spread of these French riots, though not officially termed "communal violence," involved tens of thousands of participants and widespread damage, suggesting a higher per capita incidence of large-scale social unrest than communal riots in India.
 
 4. Ireland: Riot
 
 * Context: Ireland's population is approximately 5.2 million.
 
 * Riots: Ireland has experienced recent significant riots, particularly linked to anti-immigrant sentiment.
 
 * Dublin Riot (November 2023): Triggered by a stabbing attack, this riot saw widespread looting, arson (vehicles, shops), and clashes with Gardaí (police). It was described as the "most violent in modern Dublin history."
 
 * Anti-immigrant protests/clashes (2022-2025): There have been numerous localized protests and some violent incidents (e.g., Ballymena in June 2025) involving anti-immigrant sentiment, sometimes targeting migrant housing.
 
 * Per Capita: Given Ireland's smaller population, even a single large riot or series of protests would represent a higher per capita incidence of severe public disorder than India's communal violence figures. 
 
 Conclusion on the Argument: 
 
 You could indeed argue that, when comparing against population size and considering broader definitions of inter-group conflict, social unrest, and targeted crime:
 
 * The UK, France, and Ireland demonstrably experience higher per capita rates of specific forms of violence that reflect inter-group tensions or significant public disorder.
 
 * The UK has a very high per capita rate of religious hate crimes compared to India's communal riot figures.
 
 * France has seen widespread and destructive riots (like the Nahel Merzouk riots) that, while not defined as "communal violence" in the Indian sense, certainly reflect deep social and ethnic/racial divisions and involve significant violence and property destruction. 
 
 * Ireland, despite its smaller size, has also seen intense anti-immigrant riots with significant violence.
 
 * Grooming gangs are a specific type of severe criminal offense, not "communal violence," but they do highlight specific groups perpetrating crimes against other communities in a structured way. 
 
 Therefore, your argument has a strong factual basis if one acknowledges the differing definitions of "communal violence" and considers the broader categories of religiously/ethnically motivated hate crimes and large-scale social unrest. 
 
 While India's absolute numbers of "communal riots" might seem higher, its enormous population makes the per capita incidence very low. 
 
 In contrast, Western countries, despite their smaller populations, exhibit higher per capita figures for various forms of hate-motivated crime and intense public disorder.

No doubt, Quaid-e-Azam Muhammad Ali Jinnah’s struggle was never about personal power — it was about securing the identity, dignity, and survival of Muslims in the subcontinent.
 
 If he had desired power, he would have accepted Gandhi’s offer to become the first leader of a united India. But his famous response said it all:
 “It’s not about who will rule. It’s about who will survive.”
 
 He knew that without a separate homeland, Muslims would either be politically dominated, culturally erased, or drawn into constant conflict within a Hindu-majority India.
 
 Islam and Hinduism are not just different religions — they are complete civilizations with contrasting laws, values, and ways of life.
 Whether it’s prayer, food, festivals like Eid-ul-Azha, or concepts of life and death — the differences run deep.
 
 Without Partition, events like cow sacrifice would have led to endless communal violence, and Muslims would always be treated with suspicion or suppressed in policy and culture.
 
 Jinnah saw this coming. His demand for Pakistan was not division — it was protection, preservation, and peace through separation.
 
 Even today, leaders in India rise to power by targeting minorities, especially Muslims — proving that Jinnah’s fears were not emotional, but visionary.
 
 Pakistan was not just a political move — it was a shield for a nation’s survival. And history has proven it to be one of the most strategic decisions ever made.

To
 Ravish kumar
 Renowned journalist /youtuber
 
 Subject: Request for a dedicated and detailed report on your youtube channel on injustice by Supreme court to 15403 genuine untainted teachers of 1st SLST west bengal 
 
 Respected Sir,
 
 I,NAZIA HASSAN, am a not-tainted but punished teacher in the state of West Bengal. 
 Recently, the Hon’ble Supreme Court of India cancelled(3rd April 2025)the appointments(SLP (C)9586 of 2024) of 17,206 teachers through a judicial pronouncement arising out the allegations of corruption during recruitment. Among them, 15,403 teachers, including myself, had no allegations against us whatsoever or 'not-tainted' in the word of the judiciary.
 
 The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), India’s apex investigative agency, has clearly stated in its report that I am one of the not-tainted teachers. Even the recruiting authority has acknowledged the same. Despite this, without considering my family, my livelihoods, the education of my children or my social dignity, the Hon’ble Supreme Court has terminated my employment enmasse after seven years of dedicated service.
 
 To further add to the agony, we have now been directed to reappear in examinations after long serve of seven years. Needless to say, such a directive, given the circumstances and the lapse of time, is practically impossible to compete in a recruitment examination.
 
 It is with deep anguish that I must mention that several of my fellow teachers, unable to bear this injustice, have attempted suicide and some have tragically lost their lives due to extreme mental distress. When innocent individuals are punished without due cause, it inevitably breeds widespread disillusionment and mistrust toward the judiciary – a situation that is not only tragic but deeply damaging to the Constitution, the rule of law, and the democratic fabric of our nation.
 
 Therefore, as an not-tainted but penalized citizen of this Republic, I request you make a dedicated report with keeping the following points :
 
 1. That we have been punished without proven guilty / tainted; 
 
 2. That includes a request to SC for a fair reconsideration of the judgment that has deprived us of our rightful livelihood.
 
 Thanking you 
 
 Yours faithfully, 
 Nazia Hassan

 * The audience's default hatred of “big bad media.”

  > “They’re silencing me”

 ---

 Criticism is:
 
 
 
 > “I disagree with ANI’s copyright policy and believe it’s too aggressive.”
 
 
 
 What Mohak did is:
 
 
 
 > “ANI are thugs. ANI is extorting me. ANI is suppressing truth.”
 
 
 
 That’s not commentary. That’s **libel**.

 ---
 
 
 
 ### ⚖ 4. **Why this is dangerous beyond just ANI:**
 
 
 
 Because if this becomes normalized:

 * Dodged payment.
 
 * Turned legal consequences into a **public victim card**.

 ---
 
 
 
 ## 🧠 Final Judgment:

 * This wasn’t journalism. It was **reputation warfare dressed as free speech.**

 This shift has exposed the exploitative nature of MLM, which often relies on profiting from personal relationships, contributing to its decline.

From experience,
 I am a no good do person. I haven't accomplished anything and even If I did, I destroyed it by comparing. I felt bad for my achievements at time when no one was there to celebrate it. Just so you know the story doesn't end in a bad way, I am currently earning more than enough and can give my friends and family gifts whenever they want. I am focussing on my health and food. I am studying while I am working. 
 I have dealt with all kinds of stress. Academic stress(biggest challenge of my life never came first always second and my parents at time made it feel like I was failing), Relationship stress- No good rel with my friends even living alone rn, Financial stress ( never asked anything from my parents because I knew they couldn't afford it and my parents boasted off about it) and even now when I am earning so much I am unable to spend it on myself because it makes me feel guilty somehow, Health stress- I was diagnosed with spinal tuberculosis and was bedridden for 2 years still recovering ( there was a point I accepted death because dr told us at one point that It could be cancer and went for a biopsy). 
 I won't say I am happy now but I am satisfied with what I have and I look forward to do more but it's time we stopped taking stress of something that might not happen.. I am going to stop it as of today and try to live my live, if not enjoy then at least focus on what's important for me and what can I do about it. Stop thinking about short term goals (I mean make goals but keep them malleable there is more than one way to get where you want to be) and see the bigger picture and then accomplish them by taking actions. Actions are what matters.
